notes.note: Cicero had named a colonnaded walk at his Tusculan villa "the Academy," after Plato's school outside Athens. The art he asks Atticus to send is to suit this philosophically themed space.
Cicero glosses his own Greek word gymnasiōdē: he wants pieces evoking the Greek gymnasium, the setting for athletic and intellectual culture that he was recreating in his villa.
A Greek poet in Cicero's and Atticus's circle, evidently an epigrammatist with antiquarian interests; he appears again at Att. 1.12.

The Eumolpidae were the hereditary priestly clan of Eleusis who administered the Eleusinian Mysteries. Thyillus wanted material on their ancestral rites, presumably for a poem; Cicero passes on the request to Atticus in Athens.
